chunk_summary: This section discusses diagnostic pathways for celiac disease and occult UTI as treatable causes of feeding difficulties and faltering growth in children, emphasizing screening protocols and referral criteria.
learning_objectives: Understand the diagnostic criteria and serological testing for celiac disease in children.
Learn appropriate urine collection methods and diagnostic steps for occult UTI in pediatric patients.
Recognize the importance of dietary counseling and specialist referral in celiac disease management.
Identify clinical signs that warrant screening for celiac disease and UTI in children with faltering growth.
key_facts: Celiac disease screening requires tissue transglutaminase IgA (tTG IgA) and total IgA levels; use IgG-based tests if IgA deficiency is present.
ESPGHAN criteria allow no-biopsy diagnosis for celiac disease if tTG IgA is ≥10× upper limit of normal and endomysial antibody is positive.
Urine collection for UTI screening should use clean catch or catheterization to avoid false positives from bag specimens.
Dipstick/urinalysis is a screening tool for UTI but must be confirmed with culture before antibiotics.
Recurrent or atypical UTIs require follow-up imaging per pediatric UTI guidelines.
reference_quote: ESPGHAN criteria allow a no-biopsy diagnosis in selected children with tTG IgA ≥10× the upper limit of normal plus a positive endomysial antibody test on a second sample and compatible clinical features.
document_key_topics: Celiac disease diagnostic criteria and management
Occult UTI screening and treatment in children
